name: U-shape courtyard villa silhouette
description: Overall silhouette of a building whose footprint forms a U: three rectangular wings joined at right angles to enclose a central open courtyard, with the fourth side left open to admit light, breeze and entry traffic. Characteristic of Mediterranean villas, monastic cloisters with one cloister-walk dropped, and renaissance or Chinese sì-hé-yuàn-derived palaces. The open arm typically faces south or away from prevailing winds, so the courtyard collects sun while the closed wings shelter it. The U geometry defines a strong indoor-outdoor interior space: rooms on three sides face inward onto the court, making it the social heart while the outer facades read as the building's public face. Consumed by the global_designer to fix the envelope before space_planner subdivides the wings.
placement_rules: Footprint is three rectangular wings forming a U; the fourth side is open and serves as the main entry axis.
Courtyard area should occupy 20-40% of the overall footprint bounding box — smaller reads as a notched rectangle, larger as a thin frame.
Orient the open arm of the U toward south (or whatever direction is away from prevailing winds) so the courtyard collects sun and shelter.
Rooms on the three closed wings should open onto the interior courtyard with windows, arcades or covered walkways; outer facades read as the public face.
Wing depth is roughly uniform (variation under 30%) so the U reads as a single coherent envelope rather than three disjoint buildings.
Building height is 1 or 2 storeys (5-10 blocks); taller variants risk overshadowing the courtyard and breaking the indoor-outdoor balance.

mediterranean.notes: Whitewashed stucco wings around a sun-drenched courtyard; terracotta-toned roof; arcaded walkway facing the court on all three inner faces.

renaissance.notes: Symmetrical wings with regular fenestration; ground-floor loggia along the courtyard; restrained brick-and-stone palette and a low hip cap on each wing.

chinese.notes: Sì-hé-yuàn-derived layout with the south wing dropped; lacquer-red walls, upturned eaves on each wing, central courtyard paved and planted as the household's social heart.
